title: How to Teach Your Child the 99 Names of Allah at Home
content:
The 99 Names of Allah (Asma ul Husna) are among the most treasured teachings in Islam. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said: "Allah has ninety-nine names, one hundred minus one, and whoever knows them will enter Paradise" (Sahih al-Bukhari 2736). Teaching these names to your children is not just an act of education — it is planting the seeds of a lifelong relationship with their Creator.

But where do you begin? For many parents, the idea of teaching 99 names feels overwhelming. The key is to approach it gradually, weaving the names into everyday life rather than treating it as a memorization drill. Below is a practical, age-friendly roadmap you can start using today.

Start With One Name Per Week

Rather than rushing through the list, dedicate an entire week to a single name. This gives your child time to hear it, understand its meaning, see it in action, and internalize it. For younger children (ages 3–5), focus on names they can connect to their senses: Ar-Razzaq (The Provider) when sitting down to eat, Al-Khaliq (The Creator) when exploring nature outdoors, and As-Sami' (The All-Hearing) when making dua together before bed.

Connect Each Name to a Real Moment

Children learn best through experience, not abstraction. When it rains, pause and say: "Allah is Al-Muhyi, the Giver of Life — look how He brings the earth back to life with water." When your child is scared at night, remind them: "Allah is An-Nur, the Light. He is always with you, even in the dark." When they share a toy with a sibling, affirm: "You are being generous, just like Allah is Al-Karim, the Most Generous." These small, consistent moments are far more powerful than flashcard sessions.

Use Multi-Sensory Activities

Children retain information better when multiple senses are engaged. Here are activities that work well across different age groups:

For ages 3–5: Coloring pages for each name, playdough letters to shape the Arabic, and simple songs or nasheeds that repeat the name and its meaning.

For ages 5–8: A dedicated "99 Names Journal" where they write the name in Arabic and English, draw a picture representing its meaning, and write one sentence about when they saw that name in their life that week.

For ages 8–12: Research projects where they find the name mentioned in the Quran, write a short reflection, or create a poster teaching the name to younger siblings.

Create a Visual Tracker

Print or create a chart with all 99 names and let your child place a sticker or color in each name as they learn it. Hang it somewhere visible — on the fridge, in their room, or near the prayer area. Visual progress is incredibly motivating for children. By the time the chart fills up, they will have spent nearly two years building a deep, personal connection with each name.

Make Dua Using the Names

One of the most beautiful ways to teach the names is through dua itself. The Quran instructs us: "And to Allah belong the most beautiful names, so invoke Him by them" (Quran 7:180). Encourage your child to use the names when asking Allah for things. "Ya Shafi (O Healer), please heal my grandma." "Ya Hadi (O Guide), help me do well on my test." This teaches them that the names are not just vocabulary — they are a living connection to Allah.

Be Patient and Consistent

This is a long-term journey, not a weekend project. Some weeks your child will be excited and engaged; other weeks they will resist. That is completely normal. The goal is not perfection — it is planting seeds. Even if your child only deeply learns 10 or 20 names in a year, those names will become anchors in their faith for the rest of their lives.

The most important thing is that you, as the parent, are learning alongside them. When your children see you reflecting on Allah's names, making dua with them, and connecting them to daily life, they absorb not just the knowledge but the love behind it. And that love is what will carry them through every stage of life.

title: The Best Islamic Coloring Books for Muslim Children (2026)
content:
Finding high-quality Islamic coloring books can be challenging, but it's an excellent way to combine creativity with deen.

Our top picks for 2026 focus on books that not only provide beautiful illustrations but also include educational snippets. Look for coloring books that cover topics like the Prophets, the 99 Names of Allah, and basic daily duas.

Art is a powerful medium for young minds to absorb complex concepts in a simplified, enjoyable manner.

title: 10 Ramadan Activities for Kids That Actually Work
content:
Ramadan is a magical time, and making it special for children helps instill a lifelong love for the holy month.

Instead of just focusing on fasting (which may be difficult for young ones), create a festive atmosphere. Decorate the house together, make a 'Good Deeds' jar, and involve them in preparing Iftar.

A Ramadan Activity Planner can keep them engaged with daily tasks, simple crafts, and age-appropriate learning goals, making them feel like active participants in the blessed month.

title: Why Islamic Journaling is Sunnah for Muslim Children
content:
Journaling is more than just keeping a diary; it's a tool for self-reflection (Muhasabah), which is deeply rooted in Islamic tradition.

Encouraging children to journal their thoughts, duas, and things they are grateful for helps them develop a strong connection with Allah. It teaches them mindfulness and emotional regulation through an Islamic lens.

Start with guided journals that prompt them to think about their blessings, their daily prayers, and their goals for the hereafter.
